topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The dust motes danced in the slivers of afternoon light piercing the grime-streaked windows of Grandmother’s old manor. I stood in the cavernous entryway, my hands clasped tightly behind my back, watching my brother, Mark, practically bounce with excitement. He was already cataloging antique silverware, a wide, unrestrained grin plastered across his face. I didn't move. My shoulders were rigid, a deliberate tension held deep in my spine. This entire inheritance felt like an imposition, a heavy, suffocating blanket draped over my life. "Well, look at this," Mark chirped, gesturing toward a dusty portrait. "Grandma left us this mausoleum." I finally spoke, my voice clipped and low, "It's just old wood and dead memories, Mark. Nothing special." I turned away sharply, the sound of his cheerful appraisal grating on my nerves. I needed distance, immediate and absolute.

[story 2]

I traced the peeling floral wallpaper with a fingertip, the texture rough beneath my skin. The air in the drawing-room was thick with the scent of aged lavender and decay. Mark was already downstairs, wrestling with the rusted lock on the cellar door, his laughter echoing up the staircase—a sound that scraped against my composure. I watched him from the landing, my jaw tight. Every item in this house seemed to mock me: the faded velvet drapes, the chipped porcelain figurines. I felt a distinct pulling away from everything, a desire to simply vanish into the shadows of the hallway. When Mark finally emerged, wiping sweat from his brow, he clapped me on the shoulder, his enthusiasm almost physically painful. "Come on, let's see what treasures we can find!" I flinched away from the touch, my gaze fixed stubbornly on a cobweb clinging to the chandelier.

[story 3]

The sheer scale of the house was overwhelming. I surveyed the grand, echoing rooms, a cold knot tightening in my stomach. My brother, bless his oblivious heart, was already trying to impose order, taping up loose floorboards with determined, clumsy efficiency. He beamed, convinced that conquering this sprawling property was the ultimate victory. I remained near the grand staircase, observing. My posture was deliberately slumped, a physical manifestation of my profound distaste for the situation. When Mark approached, holding up a tarnished silver locket, his voice was bright, full of undisguised glee. "Isn't this amazing? Grandma loved these things!" I didn't look at the locket. Instead, I let my eyes drift over the expanse of the empty ballroom, registering only the emptiness, the sheer waste of space. A small, involuntary shudder ran through me.
